notes: Playable form of every batch. Clues, modernCountry, ancientRegion and didYouKnow are verbatim from the source batches; `at` (real lon/lat anchors), `radiusKm` (click tolerance) and `group` (round filter) were added so the location can be found on the map. Regenerate with build_locations.py; do not hand-edit.

clues: David captured this city from the Jebusites and made it his capital.
Solomon built the first Temple here to house the Ark of the Covenant.
Jesus was crucified and rose again just outside this city's walls.
It's considered the holiest city in Christianity, Judaism, and one of the holiest in Islam.
locations.didYouKnow: The name Jerusalem appears over 800 times in the Bible, more than any other place name.

clues: Ruth gleaned in the barley fields here, later becoming the great-grandmother of King David.
The prophet Micah foretold that a ruler would come from this small town.
David was born and anointed king here.
Jesus was born here, in a manger, because there was no room at the inn.
locations.didYouKnow: Bethlehem's name in Hebrew means 'house of bread.'

clues: The angel Gabriel appeared to Mary here to announce she would bear a son.
Jesus was nearly thrown off a cliff by an angry crowd here early in his ministry.
It was such an obscure town that one of Jesus's own future disciples asked, 'Can anything good come from there?'
Jesus grew up here, giving him the name 'Jesus of Nazareth.'
locations.didYouKnow: Nazareth isn't mentioned anywhere in the Old Testament, it was a small, easily overlooked village in Jesus's time.

clues: Rahab hid Israelite spies here inside the city walls before it fell.
Joshua led the Israelites to march around this city's walls seven times until they collapsed.
Jesus healed a blind beggar named Bartimaeus here.
It's considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
locations.didYouKnow: Jericho sits below sea level, making it one of the lowest cities on Earth.

clues: Mary anointed Jesus's feet with expensive perfume here.
Jesus wept before performing one of his most famous miracles here.
Lazarus was raised from the dead here after four days in the tomb.
It sat just outside Jerusalem, near the Mount of Olives.
locations.didYouKnow: Bethany's name may mean 'house of affliction' or 'house of figs,' scholars still debate which.

clues: Jesus healed a Roman centurion's servant here without ever entering the man's house.
Peter's mother-in-law was healed of a fever here.
Jesus made this fishing town his home base after leaving Nazareth.
It sits on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee.
locations.didYouKnow: Jesus called Capernaum his own city in the Gospel of Matthew, more than any other town.

clues: Moses saw a bush that burned but was not consumed near this mountain.
The Israelites camped here for nearly a year after fleeing Egypt.
Moses received the Ten Commandments on this mountain.
It's the traditional site of God giving the Law to Israel.
locations.didYouKnow: Its exact modern location is actually debated among scholars; the traditional site sits in Egypt's Sinai Peninsula.

clues: A king here had a dream about a giant statue that Daniel alone could interpret.
Three men were thrown into a fiery furnace here for refusing to bow to a golden image.
The Israelites were exiled here after Jerusalem's temple was destroyed.
Its famous hanging gardens were considered one of the wonders of the ancient world.
locations.didYouKnow: Babylon sat along the Euphrates River, in what is now central Iraq, south of Baghdad.

clues: God called a man to leave this city and go to a land he would later show him.
It was one of the most advanced cities of its time, with a great ziggurat temple.
Abraham's family originally came from here before setting out for Canaan.
It sat near the Euphrates River in ancient Mesopotamia.
locations.didYouKnow: Ur's ziggurat still stands partially today, in southern Iraq.

clues: A prophet tried to flee from God's call to preach here by boarding a ship instead.
That same prophet was swallowed by a great fish before finally obeying.
The entire city, from the king down, repented in sackcloth and ashes after Jonah's warning.
It was once the capital of the mighty Assyrian Empire.
locations.didYouKnow: Nineveh's ruins sit across the Tigris River from modern-day Mosul, Iraq.

clues: A persecutor of Christians was blinded by a bright light on the road leading here.
That man, Saul, had his sight restored here and became the apostle Paul.
Believers had to lower Paul over this city's wall in a basket to help him escape.
It's considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
locations.didYouKnow: Damascus is still Syria's capital today, making it one of the very few Biblical cities that never lost that role.

clues: Believers in Jesus were first called 'Christians' in this city.
Paul and Barnabas were sent out from here on the first missionary journey.
It became one of the earliest major centers of the Christian church outside Jerusalem.
It sat in the Roman province of Syria, in what is now southern Turkey.
locations.didYouKnow: Antioch was once one of the largest cities in the entire Roman Empire, rivaling Rome and Alexandria.

clues: Paul lived and worked here as a tentmaker alongside Priscilla and Aquila.
Paul wrote two of his most famous New Testament letters to the church in this city.
It was known in the ancient world for its wealth, immorality, and busy trade routes.
It sat on a narrow strip of land connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese.
locations.didYouKnow: Corinth's location made it a major crossroads for sea trade between the Aegean and Ionian Seas.

clues: Silversmiths here started a riot because Paul's preaching was hurting their idol-making business.
Paul spent about three years teaching in this city, longer than almost anywhere else.
It's home to one of the seven churches addressed directly in the Book of Revelation.
It was famous in the ancient world for its massive temple to the goddess Artemis.
locations.didYouKnow: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus was considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

clues: The apostle John was exiled here for preaching the word of God.
John received the visions that make up the last book of the New Testament while here.
It's a small, rocky island in the Aegean Sea.
It's the traditional setting where the Book of Revelation was written.
locations.didYouKnow: Patmos is a real, small Greek island that still draws Christian pilgrims today.

clues: After 40 days of rain and months adrift, a boat came to rest on mountains in this region.
Noah sent out a dove and a raven from here to check if the waters had receded.
It's the traditional resting place of Noah's Ark.
It's the tallest mountain in modern-day Turkey.
locations.didYouKnow: Mount Ararat has been the subject of expeditions for centuries by people searching for remains of the ark.

clues: Moses stretched out his hand and the waters split, letting Israel pass on dry ground.
Pharaoh's pursuing army was swallowed up when the waters returned.
It marked Israel's dramatic escape from centuries of slavery in Egypt.
It separates Egypt from the Arabian Peninsula.
locations.didYouKnow: Some scholars debate whether the original Hebrew, 'Yam Suph,' refers to the Red Sea or a nearby 'Sea of Reeds.'

clues: Joshua led Israel across this river on dry ground to finally enter the Promised Land.
Naaman, a foreign commander, was healed of leprosy after washing in this river seven times.
John the Baptist baptized people in this river, including Jesus.
It's the river that flows into the Dead Sea.
locations.didYouKnow: The Jordan River is fed largely by melting snow from Mount Hermon, far to the north.

clues: Angels warned Lot to flee this city before its destruction.
Lot's wife looked back at it and turned into a pillar of salt.
God destroyed this city, along with Gomorrah, with fire and sulfur from the sky.
It's traditionally located near the Dead Sea, one of the saltiest bodies of water on Earth.
locations.didYouKnow: The Dead Sea's shoreline is the lowest point on Earth's land surface, over 1,400 feet below sea level.

clues: Joseph rose from slavery to become second-in-command over this entire land.
Joseph's family settled in a fertile region here called Goshen during a famine.
Baby Moses was placed in a basket among the reeds along a great river here.
The Israelites were enslaved here for centuries before the Exodus.
locations.didYouKnow: The river Moses was placed in, the Nile, is still the longest river in Africa today.

clues: A discouraged prophet fled here after Jezebel threatened his life.
God spoke to him not in wind, earthquake, or fire, but in a still, small voice.
This was the same mountain where Moses had received the Law centuries earlier.
Elijah encountered God here after a 40-day journey through the wilderness.
locations.didYouKnow: This mountain is also called Horeb in several Old Testament passages, the same name and place.

clues: Abraham built an altar here after God first promised him this land.
Joseph's brothers were tending flocks near here before selling him into slavery.
Joshua gathered all of Israel here to renew their covenant with God before he died.
It sat between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim in the hill country of Canaan.
locations.didYouKnow: Jacob's well, associated with Jesus's conversation with the Samaritan woman, is traditionally located near ancient Shechem.

clues: Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal to a dramatic contest here.
Fire fell from heaven and consumed Elijah's water-soaked sacrifice in front of all Israel.
The false prophets were defeated and Elijah prayed for rain to end a long drought.
It's a mountain ridge overlooking the Mediterranean coast in northern Israel.
locations.didYouKnow: Mount Carmel's name means 'garden' or 'vineyard' in Hebrew, reflecting its lush, fertile slopes.

clues: Jesus called his first disciples away from their fishing nets along this shore.
Jesus walked on the water of this sea during a storm.
Jesus calmed a violent storm here with just a word.
Much of Jesus's ministry took place around the towns lining this freshwater lake.
locations.didYouKnow: Despite its name, the Sea of Galilee is actually a freshwater lake, the lowest one on Earth.

clues: Jesus attended a wedding here with his mother and disciples.
The wine ran out, and Mary asked Jesus to help.
Jesus turned water into wine here, his first recorded miracle.
It was a small village in Galilee, not far from Nazareth.
locations.didYouKnow: Cana's exact modern location is debated, with a few different villages in Israel claiming the site.

clues: Jesus prayed so intensely here that his sweat was described as being like drops of blood.
He asked his disciples to stay awake with him, but they fell asleep instead.
Judas betrayed Jesus with a kiss in this garden.
It sat at the base of the Mount of Olives, just outside Jerusalem.
locations.didYouKnow: Gethsemane means 'oil press' in Hebrew/Aramaic, likely named for olive presses once operated there.

clues: Its name means 'place of the skull.'
Simon of Cyrene was forced to carry a cross toward this place.
Jesus was crucified here between two criminals.
It sat just outside the walls of Jerusalem.
locations.didYouKnow: The exact modern location of Golgotha is debated, with two main sites in Jerusalem both claimed by different traditions.

clues: Two discouraged travelers walked this road on the same day Jesus rose from the dead.
A stranger joined them and explained the Scriptures the whole way.
They only recognized the risen Jesus when he broke bread with them.
It sat roughly seven miles outside Jerusalem.
locations.didYouKnow: Several different modern sites have been proposed as ancient Emmaus, and none is universally agreed upon.

clues: Jesus wept over Jerusalem while looking down at the city from this hillside.
He rode down from here on a donkey as crowds waved palm branches.
After his resurrection, Jesus ascended into heaven from this location.
It overlooks Jerusalem from the east, across the Kidron Valley.
locations.didYouKnow: The Mount of Olives is named for the olive groves that have covered its slopes for thousands of years.

clues: God tested Abraham by asking him to sacrifice his son here, only to provide a ram at the last moment.
This mountain was later identified as the site where Solomon would build the Temple.
It's considered one of the most sacred sites in Jerusalem's history.
It's traditionally located within what is now Jerusalem's Old City.
locations.didYouKnow: Jewish, Christian, and Islamic tradition all treat this site as deeply sacred, though for different reasons.

clues: Abraham's family settled here temporarily on their way from Ur to Canaan.
Abraham's father Terah died in this city.
Jacob fled here to escape his brother Esau's anger, and later met Rachel at a well nearby.
It sat along a trade route in upper Mesopotamia.
locations.didYouKnow: Haran's ruins still exist today in southeastern Turkey, near the Syrian border.

clues: Moses instructed that blessings be read from one of these mountains and curses from the other.
The Samaritans built their own temple on one of these peaks, rivaling Jerusalem's.
Jesus referenced this location in his conversation with the Samaritan woman at the well.
These twin mountains flank the town of Shechem in the hill country of Samaria.
locations.didYouKnow: A small Samaritan community still worships on Mount Gerizim today, considering it more sacred than Jerusalem.

clues: Paul was born a Roman citizen in this city.
It was known in the ancient world as a center of learning and philosophy.
Paul returned here after his conversion before beginning his public ministry.
It sat in the region of Cilicia, in what is now southern Turkey.
locations.didYouKnow: Tarsus was considered one of the leading intellectual centers of the Roman world, alongside Athens and Alexandria.

clues: Paul and Silas were beaten and imprisoned here, then sang hymns at midnight.
An earthquake shook the prison doors open, but the prisoners chose not to flee.
The jailer and his household believed and were baptized that same night.
It was the first city in Europe where Paul established a church.
locations.didYouKnow: Philippi was named after Philip II of Macedon, the father of Alexander the Great.

clues: A mob formed here after Paul's preaching, dragging some believers before city officials instead.
Paul had to leave the city quickly at night to avoid the growing hostility.
Paul later wrote two letters back to the church he'd started in this city.
It was a major port city in the Roman province of Macedonia.
locations.didYouKnow: Thessalonica is still a major Greek city today, known as Thessaloniki.

clues: Paul was deeply troubled by the sheer number of idols he saw filling this city.
He addressed the philosophers here at a famous public gathering place.
Paul referenced an altar 'to an unknown god' as a way into his message.
It was the birthplace of Western philosophy and democracy.
locations.didYouKnow: The gathering place where Paul spoke, the Areopagus, still stands as a rocky outcrop below the Acropolis today.

clues: Cornelius, a Roman centurion, became one of the first Gentile believers here.
Paul was imprisoned here for two years before appealing to Caesar.
It served as the Roman administrative capital of the province of Judea.
It was a major port city built by Herod the Great along the Mediterranean coast.
locations.didYouKnow: Caesarea was named in honor of Caesar Augustus, the Roman emperor at the time it was built.

clues: Moses climbed this mountain after 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness.
From its summit, God showed him the entire Promised Land he would not enter.
Moses died here and was buried in an unknown location nearby.
It overlooks the Jordan Valley from the east side of the Jordan River.
locations.didYouKnow: On a clear day, Jerusalem and the Dead Sea are both visible from the summit of Mount Nebo.

clues: Hannah prayed silently here for a son, and the priest Eli thought she was drunk.
Her son Samuel was raised here, serving in the tabernacle from childhood.
The Ark of the Covenant rested here for centuries before it was later captured in battle.
It served as Israel's religious center before Jerusalem took that role.
locations.didYouKnow: Shiloh was the location of the tabernacle for roughly 300 years before the Temple was ever built in Jerusalem.

clues: Abraham purchased a cave here as a burial site for his wife Sarah.
Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and their wives were all eventually buried in that same cave.
David was first crowned king here before later moving his capital to Jerusalem.
It's one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
locations.didYouKnow: The Cave of the Patriarchs in Hebron is considered sacred by Jews, Christians, and Muslims alike.

clues: A prophet boarded a ship here trying to sail as far from Nineveh as possible.
That prophet, Jonah, was thrown overboard and swallowed by a great fish soon after leaving this port.
Peter had a rooftop vision here of a sheet full of animals, teaching him not to call anyone unclean.
It's an ancient port city on the Mediterranean coast, part of modern-day Tel Aviv-Jaffa.
locations.didYouKnow: Joppa is one of the oldest port cities in the world, with harbor use dating back thousands of years before Jonah.

clues: Jesus asked his disciples here, 'Who do people say that I am?'
Peter made his famous declaration here, calling Jesus 'the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Jesus said he would build his church on this confession, 'the rock.'
It sat near the base of Mount Hermon, at the northern edge of Israel's territory.
locations.didYouKnow: The site was historically associated with pagan worship, including a shrine to the Greek god Pan, before this conversation took place there.

clues: Jesus washed his disciples' feet in this room the night before his crucifixion.
He shared the Last Supper with his disciples here, instituting what became communion.
The disciples were gathered here in fear after the crucifixion when the risen Jesus appeared among them.
The Holy Spirit famously descended on the believers gathered here at Pentecost.
locations.didYouKnow: The same room is traditionally believed to be the site of both the Last Supper and the Day of Pentecost.

clues: It served as the capital of the Northern Kingdom of Israel after splitting from Judah.
Ahab and Jezebel ruled from this city, promoting the worship of Baal.
Elisha performed several miracles in and around this city during a siege.
Philip preached here after the early church scattered, and many believed.
locations.didYouKnow: Samaria was founded by King Omri, who purchased the hill it sits on specifically to build his new capital.

clues: Jesus, tired from his journey, sat down and rested at a well here around noon.
He asked a Samaritan woman drawing water for a drink, crossing a deep cultural divide.
He told her about 'living water' that would become a spring welling up to eternal life.
The well here was traditionally linked back to the patriarch Jacob.
locations.didYouKnow: Jews and Samaritans typically avoided each other entirely at the time, making this conversation especially remarkable.

clues: Twelve spies were sent out from here to scout the Promised Land.
Ten of them returned with a fearful report, causing the people to despair and rebel.
Because of that rebellion, Israel wandered the wilderness for 40 years instead of entering the land directly.
It sat on the edge of the wilderness, near the southern border of Canaan.
locations.didYouKnow: Kadesh-Barnea also later became the site where Moses struck a rock in anger, an act that cost him entry into the Promised Land himself.

clues: Jesus was transfigured on a high mountain traditionally identified as this one.
His face shone like the sun and his clothes became dazzling white here.
Moses and Elijah appeared beside him, speaking with him on this mountaintop.
It's the tallest mountain in the region, its snowmelt feeding the Jordan River.
locations.didYouKnow: Mount Hermon's peak is snow-capped much of the year, making it visible from great distances across the region.

clues: Jacob dreamed of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending here.
He woke and declared, 'Surely the Lord is in this place, and I did not know it.'
He set up a stone pillar and renamed the place, which means 'house of God.'
It later became one of two rival worship centers in the divided Northern Kingdom.
locations.didYouKnow: Bethel was originally called Luz before Jacob renamed it after his dream.

clues: Jacob wrestled all night with a mysterious figure at this river crossing.
His hip was wrenched out of joint during the struggle, leaving him with a permanent limp.
He was given a new name here, Israel, meaning 'he struggles with God.'
It sat along a tributary of the Jordan River, on Jacob's return journey to Canaan.
locations.didYouKnow: Jacob named the site Peniel, meaning 'face of God,' saying he had seen God face to face and survived.

clues: Joseph was sent by his father to check on his brothers' flocks near this town.
His brothers spotted him from a distance and plotted against him here.
He was thrown into a pit and later sold into slavery to traders passing through.
It sat along a major trade route linking Canaan to Egypt.
locations.didYouKnow: Dothan is also where Elisha's servant saw the surrounding hills full of horses and chariots of fire once his eyes were opened.

clues: Paul healed a man who had been lame from birth in this city.
The crowds mistook Paul and Barnabas for Greek gods and tried to offer sacrifices to them.
Paul was later stoned here and left for dead, only to get up and walk back into the city.
Timothy, one of Paul's closest companions, was from this city.
locations.didYouKnow: The crowd specifically called Barnabas 'Zeus' and Paul 'Hermes,' since Paul was the main speaker.

clues: Paul fled here after being stoned in a nearby city.
It served as a safer stop where Paul and Barnabas made many disciples.
It was one of the final stops on Paul's first missionary journey before turning back.
It sat in the region of Lycaonia, in what is now south-central Turkey.
locations.didYouKnow: Derbe was likely chosen partly because it sat just outside the direct jurisdiction of the officials who had Paul stoned in Lystra.

clues: Paul and Barnabas preached in the synagogue here, and a great number of Jews and Greeks believed.
The city became divided, with some siding with the apostles and others plotting to stone them.
Paul and Barnabas fled here after learning of the plot against their lives.
It was one of the main cities on Paul's first missionary journey through Asia Minor.
locations.didYouKnow: Iconium survives today as the modern Turkish city of Konya.

clues: Paul wrote a letter to the church in this city while imprisoned, though he had likely never visited in person.
The letter warned against false teachings blending philosophy and religious ritual.
Epaphras, a local believer, is credited with founding the church here.
It sat along the Lycus River valley in the Roman province of Asia.
locations.didYouKnow: Colossae was a declining town by Paul's time, already overshadowed by its larger neighbors Laodicea and Hierapolis.

clues: This church was famously rebuked in Revelation for being 'lukewarm, neither cold nor hot.'
It was told it was actually 'wretched, poor, blind, and naked' despite believing itself wealthy.
It's one of the seven churches addressed directly in the Book of Revelation.
It was a genuinely wealthy banking and textile city in the Roman province of Asia.
locations.didYouKnow: Laodicea's real water supply came through pipes from nearby hot springs, arriving lukewarm by the time it reached the city, likely the inspiration behind the famous rebuke.

clues: This church was told it would suffer persecution but to remain faithful even unto death.
It was promised a 'crown of life' for enduring hardship.
It's one of only two of the seven churches in Revelation to receive no criticism at all.
It survives today as the modern Turkish city of Izmir.
locations.didYouKnow: Smyrna is one of the very few ancient cities named in the Bible that has remained a major, continuously inhabited city right up to today.

clues: This church was described as dwelling 'where Satan's throne is.'
It was warned against followers of Balaam and the Nicolaitans within its ranks.
It's one of the seven churches addressed directly in the Book of Revelation.
It was home to a massive altar to Zeus and a famous library that once rivaled Alexandria's.
locations.didYouKnow: Pergamum's great library was so renowned that parchment itself is believed to take its name from the city.

clues: This church was told, 'You have a reputation of being alive, but you are dead.'
It was urged to wake up and strengthen what little remained before it died out completely.
It's one of the seven churches addressed directly in the Book of Revelation.
It was once the wealthy capital of the ancient kingdom of Lydia.
locations.didYouKnow: Sardis was famously captured twice in history by enemies who scaled its supposedly unassailable cliffs while its guards weren't watching, a detail echoed in Revelation's warning to 'wake up.'

clues: This church was told God had set before it 'an open door that no one can shut.'
It's one of only two of the seven churches in Revelation to receive no criticism at all.
It was praised for keeping God's word despite having 'little strength.'
It survives today as the modern Turkish city of Alaşehir.
locations.didYouKnow: This ancient Philadelphia predates the American city by well over a thousand years, and both names mean 'brotherly love' in Greek.

clues: This was one of the five major cities of the Philistines along the coast.
Samson killed thirty men here after losing a riddle bet at his wedding feast.
David lamented that news of Saul and Jonathan's deaths should not be told in its streets.
It sat on the Mediterranean coast, west of Jerusalem.
locations.didYouKnow: Ashkelon is one of the oldest and largest seaports discovered in the ancient world, with harbor use dating back over 3,000 years.
